summary: Charity Majors argues that the term 'observability' has been co-opted by vendors and executives who use it to describe basic monitoring tools, while the actual problem observability was meant to solve — understanding service quality from each individual customer's perspective — remains unaddressed by most 'observability' products. She coins the term 'o11ywashing' (analogous to 'cloudwashing') to describe this phenomenon and argues Honeycomb needs to better communicate observability's true value to executives, not just engineers.
key_points: Mainstream tech executives now use 'observability' to mean basic monitoring (up/down, metrics, logs, traces) while believing that understanding per-customer service quality is an unsolved problem requiring custom tooling
The original definition of observability — combining app, business, and system telemetry to understand quality from each customer's perspective — has been lost to marketing co-option
Legacy vendors and new entrants alike are 'o11ywashing' their products because the observability market is too lucrative to resist
The solution requires speaking to engineering executives in terms of business outcomes rather than technical details like cardinality and dimensionality
Observability should be understood as a systems problem rather than an operational one — it's about disrupting software doom spirals, not just firefighting
money_quotes: If your 'observability' tooling doesn't help you understand the quality of your product from the customer's perspective, EACH customer's perspective, it isn't fucking observability. It's just monitoring dressed up in marketing dollars.
This guy is literally describing the original definition of observability, and he doesn't even know it. He doesn't know it so hard that he went and built his own thing.
Observability is a systems problem. It's the most powerful lever in your arsenal when it comes to disrupting software doom spirals and turning them into positive feedback loops.

When Christine and I started Honeycomb, nearly ten years ago, we were innocent, doe-eyed engineers who truly believed on some level that if we just explained the technical details of cardinality and dimensionality clearly and patiently enough to the world, enough times, the consequences to the business would become obvious to everyone involved.
Companies are dumping more and more and more money into their so-called observability tools, and not getting any closer to a solution.
key_insight: The term 'observability' has been hollowed out by vendors and executives to mean basic monitoring, and fixing this requires communicating observability's true value — understanding per-customer service quality as a systems problem — directly to engineering executives in business terms, not just to engineers in technical ones.
